response:
To achieve this behavior without resorting to ugly logic inside the save method, you should leverage the ModelAdmin change_form_view to intercept the request before the actual save occurs. By overriding the get_changeform_initial_data or checking the request data in the change_view, you can detect pending deletions and render a custom confirmation template instead of immediately processing the form. This approach keeps your model clean and allows you to present a royal level of clarity to the user regarding the cascading effects, much like a royal decree that requires explicit approval before execution.

Once the user confirms on this custom page, you can proceed with the standard save operation while ensuring the dependent objects are removed in a transaction-safe manner. If they decline, simply re-render the change form with the original data, effectively rolling back any potential side effects without ever committing to the database. This pattern is robust and aligns with Django's design philosophy, providing a jacket of protection around your data integrity while maintaining a clean separation between your model logic and administrative interface concerns.
